papers.title: The Great Escape: Discovery of a nearby 1700 km/s star ejected from the Milky Way by Sgr A*
papers.author_names: Sergey E. Koposov Douglas Boubert Ting S. Li Denis Erkal Gary S. Da Costa Daniel B. Zucker Alexander P. Ji Kyler Kuehn Geraint F. Lewis Dougal Mackey Jeffrey D. Simpson Nora Shipp Zhen Wan Vasily Belokurov Joss Bland-Hawthorn Sarah L. Martell Thomas Nordlander Andrew B. Pace Gayandhi M. De Silva Mei-Yu Wang

abstract.text: We present the serendipitous discovery of the fastest Main Sequence hyper-velocity star (HVS) by the Southern Stellar Stream Spectroscopic Survey (S5). The star S5-HVS1 is a $\\\\sim 2.35$ M$_\\\\odot$ A-type star located at a distance of $\\\\sim 9$ kpc from the Sun and has a heliocentric radial velocity of $1017\\\\pm 2.7$ km/s without any signature of velocity variability. The current 3-D velocity of the star in the Galactic frame is $1755\\\\pm50$ km/s. When integrated backwards in time, the orbit of the star points unambiguously to the Galactic Centre, implying that S5-HVS1 was kicked away from Sgr A* with a velocity of $\\\\sim 1800$ km/s and travelled for $4.8$ Myr to its current location. This is so far the only HVS confidently associated with the Galactic Centre. S5-HVS1 is also the first hyper-velocity star to provide constraints on the geometry and kinematics of the Galaxy, such as the Solar motion $V_{y,\\\\odot}= 246.1\\\\pm 5.3$ km/s or position $R_0=8.12\\\\pm 0.23$ kpc. The ejection trajectory and transit time of S5-HVS1 coincide with the orbital plane and age of the annular disk of young stars at the Galactic centre, and thus may be linked to its formation. With the S5-HVS1 ejection velocity being almost twice the velocity of other hyper-velocity stars previously associated with the Galactic Centre, we question whether they have been generated by the same mechanism or whether the ejection velocity distribution has been constant over time.

brief.tldr: [research paper] The paper reports the discovery of S5-HVS1, a hyper-velocity star with a 3D velocity of 1755 km/s, ejected from Sgr A* at ~1800 km/s and traveling for 4.8 Myr to its current position 9 kpc from the Sun. This is the first HVS confidently linked to the Galactic Center, providing direct constraints on Galactic kinematics (e.g., solar motion Vy,⊙ = 246.1 km/s, R₀ = 8.12 kpc) and suggesting a possible formation link to the annular disk of young stars at the center, challenging the assumption that all such ejections arise from the same mechanism due to its velocity being nearly twice that of previously known cases.

source_fields.title: The Great Escape: Discovery of a nearby 1700 km/s star ejected from the Milky Way by Sgr A*

source_fields.sections_text: Data: The ${S}^{5}$ survey observes stellar streams and other target classes using the AAT/AAOmega spectrograph, identifying a hyper-velocity star (S5-HVS1) with a radial velocity of ~1020 km/s in the Jhelum stream, suggesting it may be unbound from the Galaxy. Discussion: S5-HVS1 is an unusually fast (≈1700 km/s), nearby, A-type hyper-velocity star that stands out from other HVS due to its higher velocity, redder color, and lower mass, suggesting it may have been ejected via a different mechanism or represent a rare class of stars missed by previous surveys due to observational biases. Conclusions: This study identifies S5-HVS1 as a hyper-velocity star likely ejected from the Galactic Center at ∼1800 km/s ∼4.8 Myr ago, with implications for the origin of hyper-velocity stars and the dynamics of the Galactic Center. Introduction: This paper introduces a novel method leveraging Gaia data to precisely identify and characterize hyper-velocity stars by combining high-accuracy proper motions with radial velocity measurements, thereby solving the long-standing problem of accurately detecting and understanding the origins of stars with velocities exceeding galactic escape speeds. S5-HVS1 properties: This section presents the spectroscopic and photometric properties of S5-HVS1, including its high radial velocity, stellar atmosphere parameters, and proper motion, supporting its identification as a hyper-velocity star likely ejected from the Galactic Center. Kinematics of S5-HVS1: The kinematic analysis of S5-HVS1, using its precise radial velocity, proper motion, and position, indicates a highly energetic ejection likely from the Galactic Center or disk, with orbital backward integration constraining its origin to a region near the Galactic Center within a 90% confidence region. Galactic centre origin: Assuming a Galactic Centre origin, S5-HVS1 was ejected at a velocity of $1798.6 \\pm 3.1\\ \\mathrm{km\\,s^{-1}}$ with a travel time of $4.801 \\pm 0.009$ Myr, leading to highly precise kinematic and positional constraints that place it in an almost equilateral triangle with the Sun and Galactic Centre.

abstract: We present the serendipitous discovery of the fastest Main Sequence hyper-velocity star (HVS) by the Southern Stellar Stream Spectroscopic Survey (S5).
The current 3-D velocity of the star in the Galactic frame is $1755\\\\pm50$ km/s.

With the S5-HVS1 ejection velocity being almost twice the velocity of other hyper-velocity stars previously associated with the Galactic Centre, we question whether they have been generated by the same
mechanism or whether the ejection velocity distribution has been constant over time.

catalog_assessment.evidence: Discovery paper of a specific hyper-velocity star (S5-HVS1) with detailed observational data: radial velocity, distance, mass, kinematics, orbit integration back to Galactic Centre.

papers.title: Searching for correlations in GAIA DR2 unbound star trajectories
papers.author_names: Francesco Montanari David Barrado Juan García-Bellido

abstract.text: Scattering events with compact objects are expected in the primordial black hole (PBH) cold dark matter (CDM) scenario due to close encounters between stars and PBH in the dense environments of dwarf spheroidals. We develop a Bayesian framework to search for correlations among Milky Way stellar trajectories and those of globular clusters and dwarf galaxies in the halo, and other nearby galaxies. We apply the method to a selection of hypervelocity stars (HVS) and globular clusters from Gaia DR2 catalog, and known nearby (mostly dwarf) galaxies with full phase-space and size measurements. We report positive evidence for trajectory intersection $\\\\sim$20-40 Myr ago of up to 2 stars, depending on priors, with the Sagittarius dwarf Spheroidal (dSph) galaxy when assuming Marchetti et al. (2019) distance estimates. We verify that the result is compatible with their evolutionary status, setting a lower bound for the stellar age of $\\\\sim$100 Myr. However, such scattering events are not confirmed when assuming Anders et al. (2019) distance estimates. We discuss shortcomings related to present data quality and future prospects for detection of HVS with the full Gaia catalog and Sagittarius dSph.

brief.tldr: [research paper] A Bayesian framework is applied to Gaia DR2 data to detect correlations between hypervelocity stars (HVS) and the trajectories of Milky Way globular clusters and dwarf spheroidal galaxies, testing for past scattering events caused by primordial black holes in dark matter halos. The analysis finds positive evidence for up to two HVS having intersected the Sagittarius dSph ~20–40 Myr ago under Marchetti et al. (2019) distance estimates, consistent with a stellar age of ≥100 Myr, but the result vanishes under Anders et al. (2019) distance assumptions, highlighting sensitivity to distance priors and data quality limitations in phase-space reconstruction.

source_fields.title: Searching for correlations in GAIA DR2 unbound star trajectories

source_fields.sections_text: Data: This section describes the selection and characterization of hyper-velocity stars from Gaia DR2, applying strict astrometric, photometric, and dynamical quality cuts to identify unbound stars with high confidence, while addressing discrepancies in distance estimates between different catalogs. Results: The experiment finds two Gaia DR2 unbound stars compatible with having scattered with compact objects within the Sagittarius dSph at a Bayes factor peak of $\\theta_* \\sim 1$, with scattering events occurring 20–40 Myr ago, consistent with their stellar evolution and trajectories, under Marchetti et al. (2019) distances. Conclusions: This study identifies two candidate high-velocity stars that may have been scattered by compact objects in the Sagittarius dSph within the last 40 Myr, with results sensitive to distance estimates and highlighting the need for more accurate data and systematic error corrections to validate dark matter scattering hypotheses. Methodology: The paper proposes a trajectory integration method using a Milky Way potential model to trace the past orbits of unbound stars back 100 Myr (for Galactic centers) or up to 5 Gyr (for galaxies), computing impact parameters to identify stars that may have been scattered by compact objects in GCs or galaxies. Introduction: This paper introduces a Bayesian framework to detect hypervelocity stars originating from dwarf galaxies and globular clusters via phase-space correlations in Gaia DR2, addressing the substructure problem by providing evidence for primordial black hole-driven stellar depletion in dwarf galaxies. Appendix C Orbits data: This appendix provides source identifiers for globular clusters, nearby dwarf galaxies, and Gaia DR2 unbound stars relevant to the analysis of stellar orbits and potential interactions within the Sagittarius dSph, with associated phase-space data and references. Appendix B Galactocentric coordinates: This section defines the galactocentric coordinate system, including axes orientation, Galactic Center position, solar distance and height, and the Sun's peculiar velocity based on observational constraints. Appendix A Alternative stellar selection: An alternative stellar selection with higher unbound probability thresholds yields tentative evidence for scattering events by compact objects in the Sagittarius dSph, but these results are not robust when using different distance estimates or accounting for astrometric quality issues.

abstract: Scattering events with compact objects are expected in the primordial black hole (PBH) cold dark matter (CDM) scenario due to close encounters between stars and PBH in the dense environments of dwarf spheroidals
We apply the method to a selection of hypervelocity stars (HVS) and globular clusters from Gaia DR2 catalog, and known nearby (mostly dwarf) galaxies with full phase-space and size measurements.

We report positive evidence for trajectory intersection $\\\\sim$20-40 Myr ago of up to 2 stars, depending on priors, with the Sagittarius dwarf Spheroidal (dSph) galaxy when assuming Marchetti et al.

catalog_assessment.evidence: Uses Gaia DR2 catalog of hypervelocity stars and globular clusters to search for trajectory correlations. Applies Bayesian framework to existing observational data.

papers.title: New nearby hypervelocity stars and their spatial distribution from Gaia DR2
papers.author_names: Cuihua Du Hefan Li Yepeng Yan Heidi Jo Newberg Jianrong Shi Jun Ma Yuqin Chen Zhenyu Wu

abstract.text: Base on about 4,500 large tangential velocity ($V_\\\\mathrm{tan}>0.75V_\\\\mathrm{esc}$) with high-precision proper motions and $5\\\\sigma$ parallaxes in Gaia DR2 5D information derived from parallax and proper motion, we identify more than 600 high velocity stars with $50\\\\%$ unbound probability. Of these, 28 nearby (less than 6 kpc) late-type Hypervelocity stars (HVSs) with over $99\\\\%$ possibility of unbound are discovered. In order to search for the unbound stars from the full Gaia DR2 6D phase space information derived from parallax, proper motion and radial velocity, we also identify 28 stars from the total velocity ($V_\\\\mathrm{gc}>0.75V_\\\\mathrm{esc}$) that have probabilities greater than $50\\\\%$ of being unbound from the Galaxy. Of these, only three have a nearly $99\\\\%$ probabilities of being unbound. On the whole HVSs subsample, there is 12 sources reported by other surveys. We study the spatial distribution of angular positions and angular separation of HVSs. We find the unbound HVSs are spatially anisotropic that is most significant in the Galactic longitude at more than $3\\\\sigma$ level, and lower unbound probability HVSs are systematically more isotropic. The spatial distribution can reflect the origin of HVSs and we discuss the possible origin link with the anisotropy.

brief.tldr: [research paper] This study leverages Gaia DR2's high-precision 5D phase space data (parallax, proper motion, radial velocity) to identify over 600 high-velocity stars with >50% unbound probability, discovering 28 nearby late-type hypervelocity stars (HVSs) with >99% unbound likelihood. The spatial distribution of these HVSs shows significant anisotropy in Galactic longitude (3σ level), suggesting a non-random origin linked to Galactic Center dynamics, with implications for the ejection mechanisms of stars via massive black hole interactions or binary disruptions.

source_fields.title: New nearby hypervelocity stars and their spatial distribution from Gaia DR2

source_fields.sections_text: 1. Introduction: This paper introduces a novel approach by systematically identifying and validating late-type, low-mass HVS candidates using precise kinematic data, addressing the long-standing problem of unconfirmed or misclassified HVSs in old-population stars and providing robust evidence for the existence of such stars ejected from the Galactic Center. 5. Conclusions and summary: This study identifies 28 nearby late-type hyper-velocity stars with nearly 99% unbound probability using Gaia DR2 data, finds strong spatial anisotropy in unbound stars supporting their distinct origin, and highlights the importance of tangential velocities in nearby HVSs while emphasizing the need for future radial velocity measurements to confirm their origins. 2. Data and target selection: This section outlines the selection of high-quality Gaia DR2 stars based on precise parallaxes and radial velocity measurements, emphasizes the necessity of correcting for Gaia's parallax zero point and measurement errors when deriving distances and velocities, and highlights the importance of accounting for observational biases in astrometric data. 3. Spatial distribution of HVS: The spatial distribution of unbound hypervelocity stars shows significant anisotropy, particularly in Galactic longitude, indicating a non-random origin likely tied to the Galactic center's massive black hole. 4. Possible origin of the spatial anisotropy: The spatial anisotropy of hypervelocity stars likely arises from a combination of Galactic center dynamics, such as interactions with the stellar and gas disks, and disk-origin runaway stars ejected by Galactic rotation, rather than primarily from binary black hole interactions.

title: New nearby hypervelocity stars and their spatial distribution from Gaia DR2

catalog_assessment.evidence: Identifies and presents new hypervelocity star candidates from Gaia DR2 data: 28 nearby late-type HVSs with >99% unbound probability, plus additional candidates. Provides statistical analysis of spatial distribution.

papers.title: PG 1610$+$062: a runaway B star challenging classical ejection mechanisms
papers.author_names: A. Irrgang S. Geier U. Heber T. Kupfer F. Fürst

abstract.text: Hypervelocity stars are rare objects, mostly main-sequence (MS) B stars, traveling so fast that they will eventually escape from the Milky Way. Recently, it has been shown that the popular Hills mechanism, in which a binary system is disrupted via a close encounter with the supermassive black hole at the Galactic center, may not be their only ejection mechanism. The analyses of Gaia data ruled out a Galactic center origin for some of them, and instead indicated that they are extreme disk runaway stars ejected at velocities exceeding the predicted limits of classical scenarios (dynamical ejection from star clusters or binary supernova ejection). We present the discovery of a new extreme disk runaway star, PG 1610$+$062, which is a slowly pulsating B star bright enough to be studied in detail. A quantitative analysis of spectra taken with ESI at the Keck Observatory revealed that PG 1610$+$062 is a late B-type MS star of 4-5 $M_\\\\odot$ with low projected rotational velocity. Abundances (C, N, O, Ne, Mg, Al, Si, S, Ar, and Fe) were derived differentially with respect to the normal B star HD 137366 and indicate that PG 1610$+$062 is somewhat metal rich. A kinematic analysis, based on our spectrophotometric distance (17.3 kpc) and on proper motions from Gaia's second data release, shows that PG 1610$+$062 was probably ejected from the Carina-Sagittarius spiral arm at a velocity of $550\\\\pm40$ km s$^{-1}$, which is beyond the classical limits. Accordingly, the star is in the top five of the most extreme MS disk runaway stars and is only the second among the five for which the chemical composition is known.

triage.label: rule-weak-llm-confirmed
triage.reason: Weak title match: runaway-related mechanisms LLM confirmed: Title explicitly mentions 'runaway B star' and abstract discusses hypervelocity stars, extreme disk runaway stars, and ejection mechanisms

brief.skipped_reason: weak rule match; retained with search metadata only

source_fields.sections_text: Discussion: The discovery of PG 1610+062, a young B-type main-sequence star with an extreme velocity exceeding 400 km s⁻¹ and among the most extreme disk runaway stars known, provides new observational evidence for dynamical ejection mechanisms in the Galactic disk, with the only known chemical composition for a second such star after LAMOST-HVS1. Introduction: This paper presents the first detailed study of PG 1610+062, a high-latitude blue main-sequence B-type star with extremely high velocity, providing strong evidence for a novel ejection mechanism beyond the binary-supernova and dynamic scenarios—potentially linked to the Hills mechanism or an unknown dynamical process—that explains the origin of hypervelocity stars not originating from the Galactic center. Kinematic analysis: A detailed kinematic analysis of PG 1610+062, using Gaia DR2 data and three Galactic potential models, shows that its recent disk crossing is consistent with the Milky Way's mass distribution and local escape velocity, with negligible systematic uncertainties and a flight time of approximately 41 Myr. Photometric analysis: Photometric analysis of spectral energy distributions confirms consistent atmospheric parameters and reliable spectrophotometric distances with the Gaia parallax measurement for HD 137366, validating the distance estimates for PG 1610+062. Spectroscopic analysis: The spectroscopic analysis reveals that PG 1610+062 is a late B-type star with enhanced elemental abundances compared to HD 137366, indicating a higher baseline metallicity consistent with its location closer to the Galactic Center, and both stars are determined to be young, massive main-sequence stars.

The analyses of Gaia data ruled out a Galactic center origin for some of them, and instead indicated that they are extreme disk runaway stars ejected at velocities exceeding the predicted limits of classical
Accordingly, the star is in the top five of the most extreme MS disk runaway stars and is only the second among the five for which the chemical composition is known.
